actions.title:
Retain and support
staff
actions.description:
Sustain a high quality comprehensive high school program.
-Maintain competitive salaries to attract and retain high quality teachers,
administrators and support staff.
-Maintain high quality teacher recruiting strategies and support teachers to
get credentials and confirm highly qualified status as necessary.

actions.title: Targeted Intervention
actions.description:
R e v ised
Continue to sustain a high quality comprehensive high school program for
all students while supplementing those opportunities with General Fund
and other resources to provide a program targeted to English Language
Learners, students who are under-resourced (SED) and foster youth
through targeted participation in Academic Foundations course sections to
improve course passage and A-G completion.

actions.title: Student Support
actions.description:
Revised
With a focus on students who are unhoused (homeless) or foster youth,
monitor student progress towards graduation through coordination
between school counselors, administrators and other school personnel,
with support from the Educational Options School Counselor, as
documented through student contacts related to counseling and
attendance.

actions.title:
ELA: Skill
Development
actions.description:
LREBG Action
The English Curriculum Lead, in collaboration with the Coordinator of
Curriculum and Teacher Leadership, continue to support course-alike
teams and deliver professional development to implement a rigorous,
guaranteed, and viable curriculum; honing students’ argumentative skills;
challenging students to read complex texts (and non-fiction, in particular);
and building students’ speaking, listening, and academic conversation
skills.
Research suggests that learning is maximized when students are
challenged just beyond their current level particularly when provided
instructional supports.
Metric being used to monitor the action: Metric 1.8
LREBG Funds supporting this action: $169,420 for 2025-26. Federal
Funds are supporting the balance of the cost, $110,686, for 2025-26.

actions.title: ELA: Instruction
actions.description:
In support of equity, social justice and increasing student engagement
through content and materials that reflect the backgrounds, experiences
and cultures of our students, professional development opportunities for
teachers will focus on ensuring high levels of learning for all students and
expanding the canon of English literature that is implemented.

actions.title:
ELA: Targeted
Intervention
actions.description:
Revised
Continue to sustain a high quality comprehensive high school program for
all students while supplementing those opportunities with General Fund
and other resources to provide a program targeted to students who are
under-resourced (SED) and foster youth, Academic Reading and Writing
(Read 180 and/or other reading support program/intervention) is
implemented as a targeted intervention in support of course passage and
A-G completion.

actions.title: ELA: Instruction
actions.description:
Revised
In support of students having access to high-level ELA in the least
restrictive environment, professional development opportunities for
teachers will focus on the durable skill of communication: speaking,
listening, and academic conversation skills. In coordination with their
performance reflected on CA Dashboard, this action is principally directed
toward our students with disabilities districtwide; under-resourced (socio-
economically disadvantaged (SED)), students with disabilities, English
Learners, and students who are Hispanic at Homestead High School;
students with disabilities at Cupertino High School; English Learners,
students who are under-resourced (SED), students with disabilities, and
students who are Hispanic at Fremont High School.

actions.title:
Mathematics:
Instruction
actions.description:
LREBG Action
Revised
In support of students having access to high-level mathematics in the least
restrictive environment, math professional development opportunities for
teachers will focus on collaborative learning to support productive struggle
and increase math discourse. Students will grow as independent math
learners, where they can think deeply, engage in discourse to make
meaning of their learning, and struggle productively. This action is
principally directed toward our Hispanic students, Long-Term English
Learners, and students with disabilities district-wide; Hispanic and students
with disabilities at Homestead High School; and students who are under-
resourced (SED), English Learners, Long-Term English Learners, students
with disabilities, and students who are Hispanic at Fremont High School in
coordination with their performance reflected on the CA Dashboard.
Research supports a focus on productive struggle in support of promoting
deeper understanding, building persistence and resilience, encouraging
metacognition, and fostering transfer of learning.
Metric being used to monitor the action: Metric 1.8
LREBG Funds supporting this action: $197,656 for 2025-26

actions.title: Science: Instruction
actions.description:
I m plement AB 285 requirements through science courses while supporting
multidisciplinary efforts to embed climate and environmental literacy
outcomes into ongoing course standards.

actions.title:
ELD: Targeted
Intervention
actions.description:
Sustain a high quality comprehensive high school program for all students
while supplementing those opportunities with General Fund and other
resources to provide a program targeted to English Learners at each
school. This action is principally directed toward English Learners at
Fremont High School in coordination with their performance on the English
Learner Progress Indicator on the dashboard. In addition, as described in
the Title III plan,
* Continue to provide workshops for teachers to examine an asset-
based pedagogical mindset for English Learners and to deepen
two key high-leverage principles for EL instruction: incorporating
all 4 language domains in every lesson and understanding and
addressing language demands.
* Curriculum Leads will continue to work with teachers to emphasize
the incorporation of the two high-leverage principles stated above
into ELD and Sheltered lessons. Also, when providing content
area professional development, Curriculum Leads will pay
particular attention to lessons that incorporate the four language
domains and the use of language objectives.

actions.title:
LTEL: Targeted
Intervention
actions.description:
Revised
Principally directed to LTELs, including those at Fremont High School,
-teachers of LTELs from different subject areas will meet during scheduled
collaboration time as a PLC. Their work will include creating and revising
curriculum, lessons and assessments and sharing strategies that support
academic language development.
-opportunities for more explicit preparation for the Summative ELPAC in
support of progress towards reclassification and on the English Learner
Progress Indicator.
-an ongoing collaboration with feeder elementary school districts to include
reading and literacy practices in support of the transition to and success
within high school.

goals.description:
All students will have access to a guaranteed and viable curriculum designed to help them pursue
their passions and interests while preparing them for college and careers, as evidenced by metrics
associated with least restrictive environment, Advanced Placement, college and career readiness.

actions.title:
Career Technical
Education
actions.description:
Principally directed for students who are foster youth and low income while
also addressing the needs of students with disabilities and foster youth in
support of progress on the college/career indicator of the CA School
Dashboard, continue to build relationships with local Community Colleges
and local businesses in an effort to increase opportunities for college credit
and work-based learning.

actions.title:
Multi-Tiered Systems
of Support
actions.description:
Continue implementation of the guidance outlined in the Student
Assistance Team (SAT) handbook to strengthen the Tier 1-3 supports at
each school site, utilizing multi-tiered systems of support (MTSS).

actions.title:
Access to General
Education
actions.description:
In support of increased access to general education for students with
disabilities, ongoing efforts and discussions of educational partners will
focus on
-Increase general and special education teacher collaboration and
implementation of accommodations, modifications and grading
adaptations.
-Integrate the principles of Universal Design for Learning (UDL) into
existing content of professional development for general and special
education teachers.

actions.title:
Advanced Placement
Courses
actions.description:
Through the collaborative work of the Counseling Assistant Principals and
District administrators, continue and expand recruitment and opportunities
for students in under-represented groups to participate in AP courses.

actions.title:
Instructional
Technology - Moved
to 3.11
actions.description:
Moved to 3.11
Provide ongoing and targeted support to teachers to expand their use of
the Schoology Learning Management System, Student Data Analytics
System, and adapting to Artificial Intelligence within education through
professional development and course-alike team coaching to integrate
technology and communication tools across content areas.

goals.description:

evidenced by the CA Healthy Kids Survey (CHKS), suspension and expulsion rates, attendance and

actions.title:
Social Emotional
Learning
actions.description:
LREBG Action
The Social Emotional Learning Curriculum Lead will continue to guide the
implementation of a Social-Emotional Learning four-year plan, student
advisories covering mental health and wellness, and provide staff training
and support.
Research from the Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional
Learning (CASEL) strongly supports the benefits of teaching students
social emotional learning (SEL): self-awareness, self-management,
responsible decision-making, relationship skills, and social awareness.
Metric being used to monitor the action: Metric 3.2, 3.14, and 3.15
LREBG Funds supporting this action: $300,000 for 2026-27. For 2025-26
the District is expending Other State Funds, $230,858 and then
transitioning to LREBG for 2026-27.

actions.title: Wellness Space
actions.description:
LREBG Action
By August 2026 the FUHSD will have a wellness space at each of its five
comprehensive campuses accessible to and inclusive of students in all
grade levels, genders, abilities, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic
backgrounds.
Research from CASEL supports a systemic approach to SEL that
cultivates responsive relationships, emotionally safe environments, and
skills development as protective factors that buffer against mental health
risks.
Metric being used to monitor the action: Metric 3.8
LREBG Funds supporting this action: $261,450 for 2025-26

actions.title:
Mental Health and
Wellness Student
Support
actions.description:
Support student mental health and wellness through access to School
Based Therapists.

actions.title: Campus Climate
actions.description:
Revised
Principally directed to support students who are Long-Term English
Learners at Cupertino High School and foster youth or students who are
under-resourced (SED) districtwide, Campus Climate group will continue to
meet monthly to norm expectations for behavior, discipline, and attendance
across schools, brainstorm and provide guidance regarding the need for
and effectiveness of interventions and engage in professional
development.

actions.title: Campus Climate

coordination with performance on the suspension indicator on the
dashboard, Campus Climate group will continue to meet monthly to norm
expectations for behavior, discipline, and attendance across schools,
brainstorm and provide guidance regarding the need for and effectiveness
of interventions and engage in professional development.

actions.title: Campus Climate
actions.description:
Revised
Principally directed to support students with disabilities at Cupertino High
School and Fremont High School in coordination with their performance on
the suspension indicator on the dashboard, the Campus Climate group will
include a representative from Special Services and continue to meet
monthly to norm expectations for behavior, discipline, and attendance
across schools, brainstorm and provide guidance regarding the need for
and effectiveness of interventions, engage in professional development,
and align practices with IEP compliance and behavior service referrals.

actions.title:
Campus Climate
Interventions
actions.description:
Revised
Principally directed to support students who are foster youth or under-
resourced (SED), while also addressing the needs of students with
disabilities, maintain the progression of interventions including drug abuse
counseling; anger management and “Strengthening Families” as
alternatives to suspension and expulsion.

actions.title: Saturday School
actions.description:
Revised
Principally directed to support students who are foster youth or under-
resourced (SED), while also addressing the needs of students with
disabilities, continue use of the Saturday School program as needed to
address absenteeism, insubordination, and disruption.

actions.title: Attendance
actions.description:
Continue and strengthen the School Attendance Review Team and School
Attendance Review Board processes.

actions.title: Facility Maintenance
actions.description: M a intain safe and updated schools through routine maintenance.

actions.title:
Instructional
Technology
actions.description:
Moved from Action 2.5
LREBG Action
Provide ongoing and targeted support to teachers to expand their use of
the Schoology Learning Management System, Student Data Analytics
System, and adapting to Artificial Intelligence within education through
professional development and course-alike team coaching to integrate
technology and communication tools across content areas.
Research consistently highlights that when students feel they are
meaningfully participating in school, it has a significant positive impact on
their academic achievement, social-emotional development, and overall
school climate.
Metric being used to monitor the action: Metric 3.1
LREBG Funds supporting this action: $418,320 for 2025-26 ($22,932 in
non-personnel and $395,388 in personnel), $436,000 for 2026-27, and
$212,171 for 2027-28. Additional non-personnel elements of the action are
being funded annually through LCFF Funds.

actions.title:
Reinforcement
Strategies
actions.description:
CS staff will utilize a wide range of reinforcement strategies at the start of
the day/1st period to increase student timely and prepared arrival to
school.

actions.title: Student Engagement
actions.description:
R e vised
CS staff will research and take student input in order to develop high
leverage/high interest field trips to increase student engagement and
academic motivation.

actions.title:
Classroom
Community
actions.description:
Partner with Community-Based Organizations to integrate programs that
provide behavioral support and leadership education (such as the FLY
(Fresh Lifelines for Youth) Law Program), creative outlets (art and music),
and parent outreach to establish and reinforce positive strategies to
support academic motivation.
